title: Surprises may surface with catch of halibut
summary: KENAI -- From the seafloor deep below a halibut fishing boat to the water surface above, there's no telling what mysterious sea creature a halibut angler might hook. "The salmon shark is probably the neatest," said Tim Kelly, owner of Ninilchik Saltwater Charters. "It's like hooking into a freight train." This summer Kelly said one of his charter's clients landed a nearly 300-pound salmon shark, one of the Pacific salmon's main predators. Salmon sharks occasionally steal bait and halibut from halibut fishermen too. But because of its sharp teeth, the salmon shark usually cuts itself free before a halibut fisherman can land it. And when a halibut fisherman does manage to land a massive thrashing salmon shark, the shark is sure to be a handful. Even on a halibut charter equipped to handle 30…

title: Steady rain brings breed of spider that's lovely, harmless and all over
summary: Under the dribble of August rain in Steve and Danielle Williams' backyard, a voluptuous creature nestles beneath the eaves showing eight delicate, striped legs and a bodacious abdomen swollen with eggs to the size of a mini marshmallow. She's an orb weaver spider, and it's almost as if she knows it's her year to be admired. A summer like this one -- with plenty of moisture and surges in moth and fly populations -- is perfect for several species of orb weavers, nonpoisonous arachnids noted for their classic, wheel-shaped webs and, at least where females are concerned, seriously juicy posteriors. Bug experts say the steady rains have driven the spiders from their usual homes, often in raspberry bushes and other greenery. They've followed insects they eat to warmer, drier digs close to houses…

title: Season now open along Turnagain Arm for small, oily fish
summary: TWENTYMILE RIVER -- As Hyong Yi carted a 5-gallon bucket of fresh-caught hooligan across the Seward Highway to his vehicle last week, his elderly mother busily used a paring knife to slice unidentified greens from a bank along the roadside. She had a plastic bag full of the parsley-shaped leaves and smiled at her bounty. Yi, an Anchorage resident, said in broken English that he didn't know what the greens were, but they would pair well with the bucket full of fish his family would be eating for the next few days. In fact, the scene along the Seward Highway from Mile 83 and south beyond Twentymile River is an impressive subsistence-gathering demonstration of how Alaskans have learned to harvest the most with the least amount of effort. Each year about this time, hooligan, a type of eulachon…

title: Discovering 3.1 million acres of hidden treasures
summary: Based on size alone, it's not easy to miss Alaska's 16 national wildlife refuges. But the lesser-known state system, which includes such designations as refuges, critical habitats and sanctuaries, also covers a lot of territory. The Alaska Department of Fish and Game's Division of Wildlife Conservation Lands and Refuges Program oversees 32 parcels of protected land, totaling more than 3.1 million acres. They are intended to preserve habitat for future generations of wildlife and marine species. Recreational activities, including wildlife viewing, photography, hunting and hiking also are encouraged. Some, such as the McNeil River State Game Sanctuary, are well known. Others, such as Egegik Critical Habitat Area off Bristol Bay, are obscure. See how many of these places you recognize. 1. Cap…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Hidden geocaches offer sport for the whole family GPS: Satellites show the location; all a cacher has to do is find it. By MELODIE WRIGHT Anchorage Daily News Published: August 23, 2006 Last Modified: August 23, 2006 at 05:43 AM WASILLA -- Jenny Weaver admits there are times when even her family says she's gone too far. But when it comes to geocaching, they're not complaining about the distance. A Palmer mom of three, Weaver emits rays of enthusiasm like sunlight. She's a person who takes everyone with her as she seizes the day. And since she discovered the sport of geocaching last spring, Weaver's day usually includes looking for stuff with a Global Positioning System (GPS) receiver. Her wanderings are family affairs that have taken the Weavers pretty far afield, and she's eager to share …
